title: Lucy I
content:
Strange fits of passion have I known:
And I will dare to tell,
But in the loverï¿½s ear alone,
What once to me befell.

When she I loved lookï¿½d every day
Fresh as a rose in June,
I to her cottage bent my way,
Beneath an evening moon.

Upon the moon I fixï¿½d my eye,
All over the wide lea;
With quickening pace my horse drew nigh
Those paths so dear to me.

And now we reachï¿½d the orchard-plot;
And, as we climbï¿½d the hill,
The sinking moon to Lucyï¿½s cot
Came near and nearer still.

In one of those sweet dreams I slept,
Kind Natureï¿½s gentlest boon!
And all the while my eyes I kept
On the descending moon.

My horse moved on; hoof after hoof
He raised, and never stoppï¿½d:
When down behind the cottage roof,
At once, the bright moon droppï¿½d.

What fond and wayward thoughts will slide
Into a loverï¿½s head!
ï¿½O mercy!ï¿½ to myself I cried,
ï¿½If Lucy should be dead!ï¿½
meaning:
William Wordsworth's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "Lucy I"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
